title: Integrity Without a Key
description:
A developer on Amaka's team proposes a design: every file uploaded to Sankofa's portal is stored alongside its SHA-256 hash, so the portal can detect if the stored copy is ever tampered with.

Amaka: 'Stop. Tell me what is wrong with that design. It sounds right and it is not. Pick the option that names the gap.'

choices: An attacker who compromises the storage layer can replace both the file and its hash together, so the hash alone gives no guarantee — you need an HMAC or a signature where the verifier holds a secret or public key the attacker does not.
SHA-256 is cryptographically broken and should never be used for integrity — MD5 is still fine for legacy systems.
The design is essentially sound, but SHA-256 should be replaced with SHA-512 because longer hashes are harder to collide against.
The gap is that SHA-256 is reversible under sufficient GPU load, so the stored hash leaks the file content to anyone with database access.
